text: The. Grabe (de Vit. p. 8.) observes that the four first verses were omitted in the Septuagint by some careless transcriber, long before the days of Origen, who restored them from the Hebrew and the other Greek versions. On such occasions his work was very useful; but the marks being soon neglected, great confusion ensued. (St. Jerome) (Kennicott, Dis. ii.)<hr>Grabe has restored these verses in his edition. (Haydock)<hr>Eusebius (Dem. x.) and Theodoret acknowledged them; and Nobilius found the first verse thus expressed in many copies, (Calmet) as it is in Grabe: "The sin of Juda is expressed in writing with a finger nail of adamant, cleaving to the breast of," etc. (Haydock)<hr>Altars, to appear more conspicuously to the latest times. This excited God's indignation. (Calmet)<hr>These figurative expressions shew the inveterate malice of the people. (Worthington)
Be left. Protestants, "discontinue from thine," etc. (Haydock)<hr>The land shall have its sabbaths, which thou wouldst not allow. (Chaldean) (Exodus 22:11., and Leviticus 26:34.)

Thus. Septuagint continue from the last chapter, "cursed," etc. (Haydock)<hr>Sedecias had formed alliances with several princes, instead of turning to the Lord, Jeremias 27., and xxxvii. (Calmet)<hr>Our chief dependence must be on God, not on human policy. (Worthington)

Tamaric. A barren shrub, that grows in the driest parts of the wilderness. (Challoner)<hr>Harhar denotes some sort (Haydock) of "useless wood." (Symmachus) See Jeremias 48:6., and Isaias 17:2.<hr>Salt, like the environs of Sodom, the fruits of which were bad.
Fruit. See Psalm 1:3. (Pindar, Nem. viii.) How different from the wicked! (Calmet)

Perverse. Septuagint, "deep."<hr>Unsearchable. Septuagint, "man, who shall know him?" (Haydock)<hr>God alone can search the heart by his own power. He enables saints to do it by the light of glory, or of prophecy; as Eliseus and St. Peter knew secret transactions. (Worthington)

Partridge. Hebrew kore, (Haydock) may mean the "coucou." The kra lays many eggs, all of which she does not hatch. (Calmet)<hr>Protestants, "As the partridge sitteth on eggs, and hatcheth them not," etc. (Haydock)<hr>It is rare that all produce birds; so the unjust do not succeed (Calmet) in the end. (Haydock)
Throne. The temple. We may also read "O high," etc., Jeremias 14:21.

Earth, where alone their portion shall be, (Apocalypse 3:5,) or they shall be forgotten. It is a proverbial expression, like that of Catullus: Dixit: sed mulier cupido quod dixit amanti In vento et rapida scribere oportet aqua.<hr>Living, which never fail, unlike those of the wicked, Jeremias 2:13.
Come. We have several instances of such impious sarcasms, Isaias 28:10., and 34:13., Amos 2:12., and Micheas 2:6. (Calmet)<hr>We fear not the Chaldeans. (Menochius)

Troubled. Hebrew, "in haste to be a pastor after thee." Chaldean, "I delay not, at thy command, to announce thy truths to them," Jeremias 1:6. Jeremias was aware of the dangers attending his office, yet not dismayed.<hr>Day: approbation, (1 Corinthians 4:3.) or advantages of the world. (Calmet)<hr>Of man. Hebrew also "of the wretched:" anush. I wished rather for the people's welfare, (Haydock) and sought not their ruin. (Chaldean) (Theodoret)

Let them be confounded, etc. Such expressions as these in the writings of the prophets, are not to be understood as imprecations proceeding from malice or desire of revenge, but as prophetic predictions of evils that were about to fall upon impenitent sinners, and approbations of the ways of the divine justice. (Challoner)<hr>The prophet has already prevented this objection, ver. 16. (Haydock)
Gate of the palace, or that by which the kings entered the temple, on the west. The time when this admonition was given is not ascertained.

Souls. Those who broke the sabbath were to die, Exodus 30:15., and Numbers 15:32.<hr>Burdens, or servile work. (Calmet)<hr>The Jews trifle in deciding what is a burden. (Origen, prin. 4:2.)<hr>Sabbath often denotes the observance of the whole law. (Worthington)

David. I will observe my covenant, 2 Kings 7:12.<hr>Horses: in prosperity, (Menochius) provided this law, as well as all the rest, be observed. (Haydock)<hr>Such temporal rewards were commonly proposed under the law, as spiritual ones are now. (Worthington)
Sacrifices. Hebrew mincha, (Haydock) "offerings" of flour, wine, etc. (Calmet)
Fire, by means of Nabuzardan. (Menochius)<hr>The city shall be entirely destroyed. (Calmet)
